results.title: Understanding steerability in AI systems
results.content:
These rules can be predefined based on domain knowledge or specified by users to ensure specific actions or constraints are enforced. This approach provides a structured way to guide AI systems towards desired outcomes.

## Challenges and limitations of AI steerability

### Data quality issues

Limited, incorrect, or biased data significantly affects the steerability of AI systems. Ensuring high-quality data is a critical challenge in building steerable AI models. Addressing these issues requires robust data management practices and ongoing monitoring.

### User context and AI fluency

Users with low AI fluency may struggle to provide accurate guidance, impacting the effectiveness of AI steerability. [...] Establishing clear objectives and guidelines is essential to overcome this challenge. Providing user training and support can also enhance AI fluency and improve steerability.

### Ethical considerations

AI steerability also involves ethical considerations, such as ensuring that the AI system does not perpetuate biases or violate legal and ethical guidelines. Addressing these issues requires careful planning and oversight.

## Future directions and potential applications

### Emerging trends and technologies

Advancements in AI technology, such as improved machine learning algorithms and better data management, promise to enhance steerability. 
Future research may focus on developing more sophisticated metrics and strategies for improving AI steerability. [...] ### User feedback and interaction

User feedback is crucial for improving AI steerability. Techniques like active learning and reinforcement learning can be employed to optimize the model based on user preferences. Integrating user interaction and feedback loops helps fine-tune the AI system's behavior.

### Data quality and bias

High-quality, unbiased data is essential for training AI models that are steerable. Poor data quality can lead to AI systems that are difficult to stee, or that produce biased outcomes.

Ensuring data quality involves rigorous data collection, cleaning, and validation processes.

### Rule-based systems and decision logic

Integrating rule-based systems or decision logic can help steer AI behavior.

results.title: STEER: Flexible Robotic Manipulation via Dense Language Grounding
results.content: × smaller model and data). Since the performance of STEER is driven by our re-annotation of behavior modes, this suggests existing robot datasets could also be re-annotated to produce more steerable action primitives, with little changes to the model architecture and training pipelines. In the future, it would be useful to scale up the discovery and labeling of dataset attributes, investigate automatic relabeling, and more directly optimize annotations to maximize the high-level agent’s skill composability. [...] For the kettle picking task, we note that the low-level policy appears to be sensitive to the specific naming of objects. That is, the VLM often produced code to grasp the ‘black and white kettle‘ from the top instead of grasping the ‘black and white object‘ from the top, and with further analysis find that this instruction has a noticeable degradation across all low-level language-conditioned policies. So, while the VLM reasonably commands the policy to grasp from above, the low-level policy is less reliable. We expect this to be improved with denser annotation or augmentation on the entity-level, whereas STEER is concerned with the motion-level. For the Fruit in Clutter grasping task, the VLM did not always command the appropriate action and we suspect that similar object naming [...] action and we suspect that similar object naming references (‘red apple’ instead of ‘apple’) impact the low-level policy performance.

results.title: Dynamic primitives in constrained action: systematic changes in the zero-force trajectory
results.content:
Mounting evidence indicates that human control is modular, composed of primitive actions (22, 33–40). Modules have been defined in different ways and at different levels of organization. One common definition proposes patterns of proportional activation of different muscle groups, i.e., synergies (41, 42). Another definition identifies subspaces of the skeletal configuration; confining motions to these subspaces achieves a simplifying reduction of dimensionality (43). Primitives have also been defined at a functional level as an action to be performed on a specific object such as grasping or pushing (39, 44–46). [...] The existence of dynamic primitives does not preclude further organization or simplification at higher levels. However, higher-level goals may be constrained by these lower-level primitives. This may explain why humans do not globally minimize energy during crank turning (71) and why they fall into a rhythm, even when trying to hit a target in a sequence of throws (99).

### Conclusions [...] 101). These limitations are important to understand the interaction between humans and the devices we develop, from complex robotic systems to simple handheld tools.

results.title: Using Vision Language Models as Closed-Loop Symbolic Planners for Robotic Applications: A Control-Theoretic Perspective
results.content: to important future research directions. The first is that we use VLMs as zero-shot symbolic planners, and we recognize that how VLMs are trained (e.g., training data distribution and training procedures) could have profound impacts on the performance of the VLM planner. Furthermore, we did not perform extensive prompt engineering in this work, and the language steerability of VLMs can also have significant impacts on the performance of VLM symbolic planners. Lastly, the action primitives used in this work are limited to pick and place, but we do expect the observations from this work to apply to VLM symbolic planning with other action primitives, since in our experiment setup we make no assumptions about the action primitives, and the VLM symbolic planner would treat other action [...] motion planner then translates each primitive action in the plan into a trajectory in the robot’s configuration space. It is worth noting that while the choice to use a motion planner as the low-level controller limits the options of primitive action, motion planners are generally robust and allow us to focus this work on symbolic planning instead of low-level control. [...] It is worthwhile to point out that in some scenarios, for instance, GPT in YCB-Hard, Gemini in Cube-Easy, and Llama in YCB-Medium, the performance of non-warm-starting variants of the CL planners completely collapses, clearly suggesting that the CL planners in those scenarios would not work without warm-starting. This result is expected, given that without warm-starting, each replanning attempt is an independent query to the VLM, and the probability of stringing together successful primitive actions decreases geometrically, and this probability decreases as the task becomes longer and the planner utilizes more frequent replanning.
